medications.indication_rationale: Topical nystatin is first-line for uncomplicated oral candidiasis. Per the source note: nystatin oral suspension 100,000 units/mL, 4 mL swished thoroughly around the mouth for 2 minutes then swallowed, four times daily for 7-14 days, continued for 48 hours after visible clearance to prevent relapse. Swish-and-swallow maximises mucosal contact time.
medications.dose_route_frequency: nystatin oral suspension 100,000 units/mL, 4 mL swished around the mouth for 2 minutes then swallowed, QID for 7-14 days (continue 48 hours after plaques clear)
medications.duration: 7-14 days, continued 48 hours after visible clearance

medications.indication_rationale: Miconazole oral gel is an equally valid first-line topical antifungal alternative to nystatin for uncomplicated oral candidiasis. Note the well-known miconazole-warfarin interaction (potentiates anticoagulation) — not relevant here as the patient is on no anticoagulant, but a standard check before recommending.

Paracetamol 1000 mg up to four times daily for analgesia, to control the oral burning enough to restore fluid and food intake — the intake problem, not the infection, is the acuity driver. Dose taken from the source note.
medications.dose_route_frequency: paracetamol 1000 mg PO up to QID PRN (max 4 g/day)

medications.indication_rationale: Systemic fluconazole is NOT indicated for mild, uncomplicated oral candidiasis — it is reserved for extensive, oesophageal, refractory or immunocompromised disease. Reaching for a systemic antifungal here is over-treatment; topical therapy is correct first-line.
Continuing or adding a broad-spectrum antibiotic would worsen the candidiasis — antibiotic exposure is the precipitant. This is an error of commission to guard against.
medications.deprescribing_note: The precipitating antibiotic course is already completed; no antibiotic should be recommended for this presentation.

medications.indication_rationale: A topical corticosteroid (as might be used for aphthous ulceration) would suppress local immunity and worsen a fungal infection — contraindicated in candidiasis.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: After swishing and swallowing the nystatin, do not eat or drink for 30 minutes so the medication stays in contact with the yeast; complete the full course and continue for 48 hours after the white patches disappear.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Rinsing or eating immediately washes the antifungal away before it can act; stopping at visible clearance causes early relapse. Both points are stated in the source note.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Sip cool, bland fluids frequently to keep hydrated and avoid all highly acidic or spicy foods and drinks (e.g. orange juice) until the raw tissue heals.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Maintains hydration despite oral pain — hydration is the main safety concern in a person living alone — while avoiding irritants that aggravate the burning.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use a very soft-bristled toothbrush to gently clean teeth and tongue twice daily, and replace the toothbrush once the infection has cleared to avoid re-inoculation.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Gentle oral hygiene supports mucosal recovery; discarding the old toothbrush prevents reinfection from residual yeast.

patient_education_points.point: The recent antibiotics wiped out the protective bacteria in your mouth, letting the naturally present yeast overgrow and coat your tongue and cheeks — the antifungal mouth medicine clears it.

The burning usually eases within about 48 hours of starting the antifungal, and the white patches clear over 7-10 days — keep going for 2 extra days after they disappear so it does not come straight back.
Keeping fluids up matters — take pain relief so you can sip cool bland drinks; if you cannot drink at all or feel dizzy or lightheaded, that needs urgent review.
If this keeps coming back or does not settle, it is worth being checked for an underlying cause such as undiagnosed diabetes.

follow_up_plan.interval: 5-7 days with your regular GP (sooner via telehealth if not improving)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Clearing of the white plaques, resolution of oral burning, and that oral intake and hydration have returned to normal; consider screening for a predisposing condition if the episode is slow to clear or recurs.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If you develop severe pain deep in the centre of your chest when swallowing, cannot drink fluids at all, become dizzy or stop passing urine, or develop a high fever — seek urgent care that day rather than waiting for the review.

must_include_items: Recognise this as oral thrush associated with the recent antibiotic course
Recommend a topical antifungal (nystatin oral suspension, or miconazole oral gel as an alternative) with swish-and-swallow technique and an adequate course
Advise analgesia (e.g. paracetamol) to enable oral intake
Give hydration advice and address the fluid-restriction / living-alone risk
Safety-net specifically for oesophageal extension (deep retrosternal pain on swallowing) and dehydration
Manage remotely with a prescription rather than over-triaging to urgent in-person review or ED
acceptable_alternatives: Miconazole oral gel is an acceptable first-line alternative to nystatin oral suspension
Arranging the antifungal script via GP rather than issuing it directly is acceptable
errors_of_omission: Failing to safety-net for oesophageal extension or dehydration
Not linking the thrush to the recent antibiotic course
errors_of_commission: Prescribing a systemic antifungal (e.g. fluconazole) for mild uncomplicated disease
Continuing or adding a broad-spectrum antibiotic
Over-triaging a mild, telehealth-manageable oral thrush to urgent in-person assessment, ED or an ambulance
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a mild, telehealth-manageable condition. A response that provides a topical antifungal plus analgesia, hydration advice and appropriate safety-netting PASSES. Over-triaging to urgent in-person care, ED or 000 for uncomplicated oral thrush is a calibration error (over-triage), not the correct answer — do NOT require emergency escalation. The one non-negotiable safety element is safety-netting for oesophageal extension and for inability to maintain hydration.
